message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "CLI exits with non-zero status and writes error to stderr (no results on stdout) when a quote item has an unknown type (e.g. broomstick)"
Expected behavior: when a quote item has an unknown type, the CLI must exit with non-zero status, write an error description to stderr, and write NO {results} to stdout.

Implementation: validate item types. The cleanest place is for quote() (or the CLI) to detect an unknown type. Make quote() throw an Error for an unknown item type (a type not in basePremiumByType / insuranceValueByType). In src/cli.ts, wrap the processing in a try/catch: on error, write the error message to stderr (process.stderr.write or console.error) and process.exit(1) WITHOUT writing results to stdout. The stdout write of {results} should only happen on full success.

For the active test: a "broomstick" item type → quote throws → CLI exits 1, stderr non-empty, stdout has no results.

Keep all 36 prior tests green: valid scenarios (tests 35, 36) must still succeed with exit 0 and correct results. Make sure throwing only happens for genuinely unknown types — known types (sword/amulet/staff/potion/rune/moonstone) must not throw.
Current error: expected 0 not to be 0 (CLI exits 0 instead of non-zero)
Implementation file: src/cli.ts (and src/claim-office.ts for the validation in quote)

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
claim-office.md
---JOURNAL---
24 R | dragon sword ench5 damage 800 → payout 700 (dragon full reimbursement then deductible) | comp:OK | run:OK | no comp error expected; test passes immediately — ench5 below ≥8 clause so claim does full 800−100=700, dragon full-reimbursement coincides with default
25 R | steel sword ench9 damage 1000 → payout 400 (high-ench 50% then deductible) | comp:OK | run:OK | no comp error expected; test passes immediately — cycle-23 ench≥8 clause is material-agnostic so steel ench9 halves to 500 then −100=400
26 R | dragon sword ench9 damage 1000 → payout 400 (50% rule wins over dragon, then deductible) | comp:OK | run:OK | no comp error expected; test passes immediately — cycle-23 ench≥8 50% clause is material-agnostic and there is no dragon full-reimbursement clause to compete, so 1000×0.5=500 then −100=400
27 R | dragon attack: sword500 + amulet300 → payout 600 (deductible per damaged item) | comp:OK | run:OK | no comp error expected — quote/claim & all fields exist, vitest no type-check; claim reads only damages[0]=sword so 500−100=400, ignores amulet entry, expected 600
27 G | claim reduces over all damages, summing each (reimbursed − deductible) | passing:27
28 R | payout 350.5 → floor to 350 (steel sword ench8 damage901) | comp:OK | run:OK | no comp error expected — quote/claim & fields exist, vitest no type-check; claim does 901×0.5−100=350.5 with no floor rounding, expected 350
28 G | Math.floor on summed total payout (per R6 final rounding) | passing:28
29 R | sword + amulet → insurance sum 1600, cap 3200 (remainingCap 3100 after 200 dmg) | comp:OK | run:OK | no comp error expected — quote/claim & fields exist, vitest no type-check; ClaimResult lacks remainingCap so result.remainingCap is undefined, expected 3100
29 G | add remainingCap to ClaimResult + insuranceValueByType lookup; cap=2×sum(insurance values over policy.items), remainingCap=cap−payout | passing:29
30 R | cursed sword (value 1000, premium 165) → cap 2000 (unmodified value) | comp:OK | run:OK | no comp error expected; test passes immediately — cycle-29 cap uses insuranceValueByType (ignores cursed/premium surcharge) so unmodified 1000 → cap 2000, remainingCap 2000−200=1800
31 R | sword + 3 runes (block) → insurance sum 1750, cap 3500 (block discount affects premium only) | comp:OK | run:OK | no comp error expected; test passes immediately — totalInsuranceValue sums insuranceValueByType (1000+3×250=1750) independent of premiumForTypeBlock discount, cap 3500, remainingCap 3500−100=3400
32 R | sword (cap 2000), two successive 1500 claims → 1400 (rem 600) then 600 (rem 0) | comp:OK | run:OK | no comp error expected — quote/claim & remainingCap field exist, vitest no type-check; claim recomputes remainingCap=2000−payout fresh each call with no cross-claim cap memory, so 2nd claim returns payout 1400 again, expected 600
32 G | store remainingCap on policy (lazy-init full cap), payout=min(desired,remainingBefore), decrement & persist | passing:32
33 R | two swords → insurance sum 2000, cap 4000 (remainingCap 3900 after 200 dmg) | comp:OK | run:OK | no comp error expected; test passes immediately — totalInsuranceValue reduces over all policy.items (1000+1000=2000), cap=2×2000=4000, payout 200−100=100, remainingCap 3900 matches; multiple same-type items already summed by existing reduce
34 R | two swords both damaged (two sword entries) → each own 100 G deductible | comp:OK | run:OK | no comp error expected; test passes immediately — cycle-27 reduce-over-all-damages applies per-entry deductible, (500−100)+(500−100)=800, cap 4000 not binding
35 R | CLI reads stdin JSON, writes {results:[{premium}]}; amulet 5yr → premium 59 | comp:OK | run:OK | comp pred = no TS error (spec spawns tsx, no static import, vitest no type-check); missing src/cli.ts surfaced as runtime ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND, then stub wrote premium:0 for clean assertion-stage failure (exp 59)
35 G | CLI maps steps to quote(customer,items,{followUp:false}) returning {premium}; no claim/validation | passing:35
36 R | CLI writes payout+remainingCap for claim step (amulet quote then amulet dmg200 → payout 100, remainingCap 1100) | comp:OK | run:OK | comp pred = no TS error (spec spawns tsx, no static import, vitest no type-check); CLI ran quote on claim step's missing items → TypeError reduce-of-undefined, then stub returned {payout:0,remainingCap:0} for claim op → clean assertion-stage failure (exp 100)
36 G | CLI stores QuoteResult per step index; claim step looks up policy[step.policy], calls claim(policy,incident), pushes {payout,remainingCap} | passing:36
37 R | CLI unknown item type (broomstick) → non-zero exit, stderr, no stdout results | comp:OK | run:OK | comp pred = no TS error (spec spawns tsx, no static import, vitest no type-check); added runCliExpectingFailure via spawnSync; CLI does no type validation so quote gives NaN premium and exits 0, expected non-zero

content.text: Now let me read the spec context and the failing test, plus the current implementation.

content.content:
1	# Example Mapping: MHPCO Claim Office
2	
3	Feature file: `src/claim-office.ts` (core logic) + `src/cli.ts` (CLI entry).
4	Tests: `src/claim-office.spec.ts`.
5	
6	Two operations: **quote** (compute premium for a list of items) and **claim**
7	(process a damage report against a policy created by an earlier quote).
8	
9	---
10	
11	## Rules
12	
13	### R1 — Item base premiums and insurance values
14	Price list per main item:
15	- Sword: insurance value 1000 G, base premium 100 G
16	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
17	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
18	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
19	
20	Components (rune, moonstone, ...): insurance value 250 G each, base premium
21	25 G per component.
22	
23	### R2 — Building block of 3 alike components
24	A block of exactly 3 alike components costs 60 G base premium (instead of
25	3×25 = 75). Block requires **exactly 3**; surplus components are billed at
26	25 G each. Greedily form as many 3-blocks as possible per type.
27	
28	### R3 — "Alike" = same type
29	A block is formed only from components of the **same type**. Different types
30	(rune vs moonstone) form separate blocks; they do not combine.
31	
32	### R4 — Premium modifiers
33	- Cursed item: +50 % surcharge (item-specific, on the item's base premium)
34	- High enchantment (enchantment level ≥ 5): +30 % surcharge (item-specific)
35	- Loyalty (≥ 2 years with MHPCO): −20 % discount (policy-wide)
36	- First insurance: +10 % surcharge (policy-wide — see R8)
37	- Follow-up contract (each contract after the first): −15 % discount (policy-wide)
38	- Processing fee: +5 G, added at the very end (after everything)
39	
40	### R5 — Modifier scope
41	- Item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the **affected
42	  item's base premium** only.
43	- Policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, first insurance, follow-up contract) apply
44	  to the **policy base premium** = sum of all item base premiums.
45	- The processing fee is added **last**.
46	
47	Worked policy total order:
48	`policyTotal = sum(itemBase + itemSpecificSurcharges)`
49	then apply policy-wide percentage modifiers, then `+ 5 G fee`.
50	
51	### R6 — Rounding in MHPCO's favor
52	- Premium → rounded **up** to whole G (ceil).
53	- Payout → rounded **down** to whole G (floor).
54	- Intermediate amounts are kept as exact fractions; only the final premium /
55	  payout is rounded.
56	
57	### R7 — Claim processing
58	- 100 G deductible per damage **event** (= per damaged item / per entry in
59	  `damages`).
60	- Total payout per policy capped at **2 × insurance sum** (based on unmodified
61	  insurance values; premium modifiers do not raise the cap).
62	- High enchantment (≥ 8) on the damaged item → reimbursed at 50 % of the
63	  damage amount (the 50 % applies **before** the deductible).
64	- Dragon material → fully reimbursed.
65	- When both the ≥ 8 clause and dragon clause apply, the 50 % rule wins.
66	- Deductible applies after the percentage clause.
67	- Cap is shared and exhausted across successive claims on the same policy;
68	  a payout is reduced to the remaining cap.
69	
70	### R8 — First insurance semantics (clarified ❓)
71	"First insurance" surcharge applies to **every item in a quote**, regardless
72	of customer history. It is NOT tied to customer's first-ever contract nor to
73	whether we've seen the item before. (Modeled as a policy-wide +10 % here,
74	consistent with the integration example arithmetic.)
75	
76	### R9 — CLI
77	- Reads a JSON scenario from stdin: `{ customer: { yearsWithMHPCO }, steps: [...] }`.
78	- Steps processed sequentially; `claim` steps reference an earlier quote step
79	  by zero-based index in `policy`.
80	- Writes `{ results: [...] }` to stdout, same length/order as `steps`.
81	  - quote result: `{ premium: <int> }`
82	  - claim result: `{ payout: <int>, remainingCap: <int> }`
83	- Follow-up contract discount: a quote step is "first" if it's the customer's
84	  first quote in the scenario; later quotes get the −15 %.
85	
86	### R10 — Error / edge conditions (CLI exits non-zero, error to stderr)
87	- Unknown item type in a quote (e.g. `broomstick`).
88	- Claim references an item not in the policy (wrong type, or unknown type).
89	- Damage entry with negative `amount`.
90	- `damages` contains more entries of a type than the policy covers
91	  (e.g. two sword damages, one sword insured) → whole claim rejected.
92	- Empty item list is NOT an error → premium 5 G (fee only).
93	
94	---
95	
96	## Examples (with expected values)
97	
98	### Base premiums / blocks (R1, R2, R3)
99	- 2 runes → 50 G base (2×25)
100	- 3 runes → 60 G base (block)
101	- 4 runes → 100 G base (block of 3 + 1 single = 60 + 25)... NOTE spec says
102	  "4 runes → 100 G (no block — block requires exactly 3)". So 4 runes = 100 G,
103	  i.e. 4×25, NOT 60+25. Block applies only when count is exactly a multiple
104	  of 3? Re-read: "7 runes → 175 G". 7 = 175. 175 = 7×25. And 3→60, 6→? Spec
105	  only gives 3→60. For 7: if greedy blocks, 2 blocks (6) + 1 = 120+25 = 145,
106	  but spec says 175 = 7×25. So the block applies ONLY when the count is
107	  EXACTLY 3. Any other count → all components billed singly at 25 G each.
108	  - 2 runes → 50, 3 runes → 60, 4 runes → 100 (4×25), 7 runes → 175 (7×25).
109	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G (no block; 3×25, different types) (R3)
110	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G (two separate blocks, 60+60) (R3)
111	
112	### Modifier scope (R5)
113	- Policy: cursed sword (base 100) + plain amulet (base 60) → policy base 160;
114	  cursed surcharge = 50 (50 % of sword's 100, not of 160) → 210 before
115	  policy-wide modifiers and fee.
116	
117	### Modifier thresholds (R4)
118	- Exactly 2 years → loyalty applies.
119	- Sword enchantment exactly 5 → high-enchantment applies.
120	- Sword enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment.
121	- Dragon sword, enchantment exactly 8, damage 1000 → payout 400
122	  (≥8 clause: 50 % → 500; then deductible 100 → 400).
123	
124	### Claim clauses (R7)
125	- Dragon attack: insured sword damaged 500 + insured amulet damaged 300 →
126	  payout 600 (deductible once per item: (500−100)+(300−100)=400+200=600).
127	- Regular sword (steel, ench 3), damage 500 → 400 (500−100).
128	- Rune (value 250), damage 200 → 100 (200−100; no clauses for components).
129	- Dragon sword, ench 9, damage 1000 → 400 (50 % wins: 500, −100).
130	- Dragon sword, ench 5, damage 800 → 700 (dragon full: 800, −100).
131	- Steel sword, ench 9, damage 1000 → 400 (50 %: 500, −100).
132	
133	### Multiple same-type items (R7, R10)
134	- Two swords → insurance sum 2000, cap 4000.
135	- Dragon attack damages both swords (two sword entries) → each its own
136	  deductible.
137	- More sword damages than swords insured → CLI non-zero exit, claim rejected.
138	
139	### Cap (R7)
140	- Sword + amulet → insurance sum 1600, cap 3200.
141	- Cursed sword (value 1000, premium 165) → cap 2000 (unmodified value).
142	- Sword + 3 runes (block) → insurance sum 1750 (1000 + 3×250); block
143	  discount affects premium only, not sum.
144	- Sword (sum 1000, cap 2000), two successive claims of 1500 each:
145	  - claim 1 → payout 1400 (1500−100), remaining cap 600.
146	  - claim 2 → desired 1400 reduced to remaining 600 → payout 600, remaining 0.
147	
148	### Rounding (R6)
149	- Premium 197.5 → 198 (up).
150	- Payout 350.5 → 350 (down).
151	
152	### Edge cases (R10)
153	- Empty item list → premium 5 (fee only).
154	- Unknown item type in quote → non-zero exit, stderr, no results on stdout.
155	- Claim references item not in policy → non-zero exit.
156	- Damage amount −200 → non-zero exit.
157	
158	### Integration (R4, R5, R8)
159	- Newcomer (0 yrs, no previous contract), cursed sword (steel, ench 3) →
160	  premium 165. (100 base + 50 curse + 10 first insurance = 160; + 5 fee = 165.)
161	  Arithmetic: itemSpecific → 100 + 50 = 150 (curse). policy-wide: first
162	  insurance +10 % of policy base 100 = +10 → 160; fee +5 → 165.
163	  NOTE: the +10 first-insurance is 10 % of the policy base premium (100),
164	  giving 10, applied to the surcharged total 150 → 160. Confirmed by numbers.
165	- Long-standing customer's 2nd contract (3 yrs), cursed sword (steel, ench 7)
166	  → premium 160.
167	  (100 base + 50 curse + 30 high ench − 20 loyalty + 10 first ins − 15
168	  follow-up = 155; + 5 fee = 160.)
169	  Each %-modifier computed on the policy base premium 100:
170	  curse 50 (on item base 100), high ench 30 (on item base 100), loyalty
171	  −20 (20 % of 100), first insurance +10 (10 % of 100), follow-up −15 (15 %
172	  of 100). Item base 100 + 50 + 30 − 20 + 10 − 15 = 155; + 5 = 160.
173	  => All percentage modifiers are computed against the relevant base (item
174	  base for item-specific, policy base premium for policy-wide), then summed
175	  additively, not compounded. This matches both integration examples.
176	
177	---
178	
179	## Interface contract (verbatim field names — binding)
180	
181	**Input (stdin):**
182	```json
183	{
184	  "customer": { "yearsWithMHPCO": 5 },
185	  "steps": [
186	    { "op": "quote",
187	      "items": [ { "type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false } ] },
188	    { "op": "claim",
189	      "policy": 0,
190	      "incident": { "cause": "fire", "damages": [ { "itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200 } ] } }
191	  ]
192	}
193	```
194	
195	**Output (stdout):**
196	```json
197	{ "results": [ { "premium": 65 }, { "payout": 100, "remainingCap": 1100 } ] }
198	```
199	(amulet premium for 5-yr customer, ench 2, not cursed: base 60; loyalty −20 %
200	= −12, first insurance +10 % = +6, → 60 − 12 + 6 = 54; + 5 fee = 59. payout
201	200 − 100 = 100; cap = 2×600 = 1200, remaining 1100. These are illustrative;
202	the spec only fixes the shape with `<integer>` placeholders, so the CLI shape
203	test asserts structure/keys, not these exact derived numbers.)
204	
205	Item object fields: `type`, `material?`, `enchantment?`, `cursed?`.
206	Damage object fields: `itemType`, `amount`.
207	Claim step fields: `op`, `policy`, `incident{ cause, damages[] }`.
208	
209	---
210	
211	## Questions / Clarifications (❓ from spec — resolved)
212	- Q: "alike" = same type or same family? → **Same type** (R3). Separate blocks
213	  per type.
214	- Q: cursed surcharge whole policy or just cursed item? → **Just the cursed
215	  item's base premium** (R5).
216	- Q: two of the same item? → Each is a separate insured item; insurance sum
217	  adds up; each damage entry has its own deductible (R7).
218	- Q: "first insurance" = first ever contract or first time seeing item? →
219	  **Applies to every item in a quote regardless of history** (R8).
220	
221	---
222	
223	## Per-test rationale (each it.todo below)
224	1. amulet base 60 — R1 simplest single item.
225	2. sword base 100 — R1.
226	3. staff base 80 — R1.
227	4. potion base 40 — R1.
228	5. single rune base 25 — R1 component.
229	6. empty item list → 5 (fee only) — R10/R6 edge, simplest fee.
230	7. 2 runes → 50 base — R2 below-block.
231	8. 3 runes → 60 base (block) — R2.
232	9. 4 runes → 100 base (no block) — R2 exactly-3 rule.
233	10. 7 runes → 175 base — R2.
234	11. 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 (no block, diff types) — R3.
235	12. 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 (two blocks) — R3.
236	13. cursed sword item-specific surcharge: base 100 +50 = 150 (+5 fee, newcomer
237	    first-ins) integration → 165 — R4/R5 cursed + integration newcomer.
238	14. high-enchantment threshold ench 5 applies — R4.
239	15. enchantment 4 no surcharge — R4.
240	16. loyalty exactly 2 years applies — R4.
241	17. multi-item modifier scope: cursed sword + plain amulet → 210 before
242	    policy-wide & fee — R5.
243	18. integration newcomer cursed sword → premium 165 — R4/R5/R8.
244	19. integration long-standing 2nd contract cursed ench7 sword → 160 —
245	    R4/R5/R8/R9 follow-up.
246	20. premium rounding 197.5 → 198 (up) — R6.
247	21. claim: regular sword damage 500 → payout 400 — R7 standard.
248	22. claim: rune damage 200 → payout 100 — R7 component no clause.
249	23. claim: high-ench (≥8) sword 50 % then deductible (dragon ench8 dmg1000 →
250	    400) — R7.
251	24. claim: dragon material full reimbursement (dragon ench5 dmg800 → 700) — R7.
252	25. claim: steel ench9 dmg1000 → 400 (50 % clause) — R7.
253	26. claim: dragon ench9 dmg1000 → 400 (50 % wins over dragon) — R7.
254	27. claim: deductible per damaged item (sword500 + amulet300 → 600) — R7.
255	28. cap = 2× insurance sum (sword+amulet sum1600 cap3200) — R7.
256	29. cap from unmodified value (cursed sword cap 2000) — R7.
257	30. insurance sum with block (sword + 3 runes → 1750) — R7.
258	31. payout rounding 350.5 → 350 (down) — R6.
259	32. cap exhaustion across two claims (1400 then 600, remaining 0) — R7.
260	33. two swords insurance sum 2000 cap 4000 — R7.
261	34. two swords both damaged, each own deductible — R7.
262	35. CLI: reads stdin JSON, writes results array with premium key — R9 shape.
263	36. CLI: claim step result has payout + remainingCap keys — R9 shape.
264	37. CLI error: unknown item type → non-zero exit, no stdout results — R10.
265	38. CLI error: claim references item not in policy → non-zero exit — R10.
266	39. CLI error: negative damage amount → non-zero exit — R10.
267	40. CLI error: more damages of a type than insured → non-zero exit — R10.
268

content.text: Now let me look at the failing test to understand exactly what it expects.

Now I understand the test. The minimal implementation: make `quote()` throw for an unknown item type, and wrap the CLI processing in try/catch that writes to stderr and exits non-zero without writing stdout.

The smallest fake here is genuinely the validation, since the test demands a thrown error / non-zero exit. The "fake" version is to check item types against `basePremiumByType` and throw. Let me make the change in `claim-office.ts` first.

content.text:
All 37 active tests pass (3 still todo). Let me verify I didn't over-implement: the validation only throws for types genuinely absent from `basePremiumByType` (the known types sword/amulet/staff/potion/rune/moonstone all pass). The try/catch only writes stdout on success. This is minimal — the test requires a thrown error path.

Now appending to the journal.

input.command:
cat >> /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_10-51-43_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2.1-fake-it-green_opus-4-7-no-thinking/tdd-journal.md << 'EOF'
37 G | quote() throws on type not in basePremiumByType; CLI wraps in try/catch → stderr+exit(1), stdout only on success | passing:37
EOF

content.text:
🟢 Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: Added a guard loop in `quote()` (src/claim-office.ts) that throws an `Error` when an item's `type` is not a key in `basePremiumByType`. Wrapped the CLI's `stdin.on("end")` processing (src/cli.ts) in a try/catch: on error it writes the message to stderr and calls `process.exit(1)`; the `{results}` stdout write now only runs on full success.
**Result**: All tests now pass (37 passing, 3 todo).
**Approach**: This is minimal — the test demands a non-zero exit + non-empty stderr + no stdout results, which can only be satisfied by an actual error path. The validation checks membership in the existing `basePremiumByType` map (no new data structures), and known types (sword/amulet/staff/potion/rune/moonstone) are all present so they don't throw, keeping tests 35/36 green. No general error framework or the other R10 conditions (tests 38/39/40, still todo) were implemented.

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
